Recent studies on digital mental health for youth

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Chelsey Wilks, PhD
11mo
  • Report this post

🧠 What’s New in Digital Mental Health Research  To stay current with digital mental health research, I’ve identified a handful of recent studies that offer practical and timely insights for anyone working at the intersection of mental health, technology, and care delivery, particularly for youth and individuals with complex clinical needs. Here are a few that stood out: 📍 1. Early engagement matters 🔗 https://lnkd.in/g37_AKSh Among young people using an unguided anxiety self-help app, those who completed more tasks early on were more likely to keep using the app, regardless of their initial anxiety severity. Why it matters: Focusing on onboarding and early interaction, through personalization, reminders, or gamification, can improve long-term engagement and outcomes. 📍 2. Combining digital tools with face-to-face therapy improves results 🔗 https://lnkd.in/gqVy_4xb An RCT found that adding a mobile app to traditional CBT was more effective than CBT alone for key clinical outcomes in college students. Why it matters: Blended care approaches can extend the reach and effectiveness of therapy, especially for young adults. 📍 3. AI should be transparent and support, not replace, human connection 🔗 https://lnkd.in/gUfZ997n  Young people find AI-driven resource recommendations acceptable only when it’s clear the interaction isn’t human, preserving the value of real peer and clinician support. Why it matters: Use AI thoughtfully, as a tool for signposting and support, with clear communication about data use and without mimicking empathy. 📍 4. Social media recruitment can be faster, cheaper, and more inclusive 🔗 https://lnkd.in/gcZ6e6sU Recruiting participants via social media was quicker, more cost-effective, and reached a more diverse group, including higher-risk individuals, compared to traditional methods. Why it matters: Leveraging targeted video ads, detailed landing pages, and pixel tracking can help researchers and programs reach underserved and vulnerable populations more effectively. If you want to discuss these findings or digital mental health trends, feel free to connect. #DigitalHealth #MentalHealthResearch #YouthMentalHealth #CBT #BlendedCare

The Role of Early Engagement in a Self-Directed, Digital Mental Health Intervention for Adolescent Anxiety: Moderated Regression Analysis - PubMed pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

    🔎 Internet Use is Associated with Better Memory and Thinking Skills in Middle and Older Age 🧠 New research in npj Mental health Research spanning 108,000 adults across 32 countries reveals a consistent pattern: internet use correlates with better memory, executive function, and cognitive performance in middle and older age. The bottom line: Teaching older adults how to get online has little downside and plenty of practical benefits. Even if we're not sure it protects the brain, it helps people stay connected and engaged.     Friday, October 31 from The Chicago School "Autistic Centered Therapy: Mood Disorders and Other Co-occuring Diagnosis" presented by Kate McNulty, LCSW and Matt Lowry, LPP https://lnkd.in/esxYmeKi The growth of the Neurodiversity movement has brought new awareness to the consulting room, as therapists increasingly encounter neurominority clients in their practice settings. While therapists may stay current with this influx of terminology and concepts, true expertise requires a shift in mindset.   Taught by two therapists who are Autistic, this course will go beyond adopting affirming language to understanding more about the internal experience of Autistic people at various stages of development. Participants will learn methods to deepen rapport with Autistic clients through relevant and incisive questioning. Systematic literature reviews support subjective clinical perceptions that Autism encompasses an array of diagnostic phenomena (Nadeem et al., 2021). Clinicians can deliver more effective therapies when alerted to accurate multiple diagnoses, but require training to discern these underlying traits, especially when clients are alexithymic or have other communication difficulties. Conditions such as ADHD (Leitner, 2014) , trauma (Golan et al., 2021), anxiety and depression (Montaser et al., 2023), sleep disorders (Xavier, 2022) and OCD (Martin et al., 2020) often accompany Autism, often these diagnoses require an urgent response before addressing any quality of life issues rooted in Autism. During this session, Participants will learn to prioritize with these complex cases to effectively relieve immediate distress and better engage clients in therapy for lasting results. Specifically, participants will learn various questions to determine degree and type of co-occurring diagnoses and selection of appropriate questions based upon client needs as well as how to determine client risk factors related to co-occurring diagnoses Finally, participants will learn to employ psychoeducational methods to integrate diagnostic understanding into treatment. #ContinuingEducation #CEs #CEUs #Counseling #Psychology #Autism

    𝗪𝗲 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗸 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗽𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝗳 "𝘄𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴" 𝗶𝗻 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗿𝗻𝘀. On paper, it sounds reasonable: let's not overdiagnose, let's avoid labeling too early, let's give development time to unfold naturally. In practice, it means parents are presenting concerns at 18 months and being told to come back at age 5. What happens in those 3.5 years? Parents are: * Managing escalating behaviors without support * Fighting school systems for accommodations without diagnoses * Implementing strategies without professional guidance * Doubting their own observations * Watching their child struggle daily * Feeling gaslit by "all kids develop differently" Meanwhile, research on early intervention consistently shows better outcomes when we act sooner, not later. Neuroplasticity is highest in younger years. Behavioral patterns are more malleable earlier. Support needs are often less intensive when addressed early. So why do we default to "wait and see"? Fear of overdiagnosis? Parents aren't asking for labels - they're asking for help. Developmental variation? Yes, development varies. But struggling across multiple environments isn't typical variation. Diagnostic clarity? You don't need a perfect diagnosis to start supportive interventions. Here's what I've learned: when parents present concerns, they've usually been observing for months before the appointment. They're not reacting to one incident - they're reporting patterns. When we say "wait and see," we're not being cautiously optimistic. We're dismissing longitudinal observational data in favor of... hoping things improve on their own? The shift in my practice: "Wait and see if concerns resolve" became "Let's intervene supportively while we assess." You can provide: * Parent training * School consultation * Therapeutic support * Environmental modifications * Strategy coaching ...without a formal diagnosis. You can help NOW while you gather more data. "Wait and see" should mean "let's monitor closely with support systems in place," not "come back when it's bad enough to be undeniable." To parents: your urgency is clinically justified. Early intervention research supports your instincts. To providers: what would need to shift in our practices to respond to parent concerns earlier?

  • Christopher Harrison, J.D., Ph.D.
    7mo

    In "AI Will Transform Neuropsychology Through Mental Health Digital Twins for Dynamic Mental Health Care, Especially for ADHD," Neil Natarajan, Sruthi Viswanathan, Xavier Roberts-Gaal, and Michelle Marie Martel argue that artificial intelligence could revolutionize mental health diagnostics by moving from static snapshots to continuously evolving models of the human mind. The authors propose “mental health digital twins” that track each patient’s symptoms and behaviors in real time—potentially reshaping how conditions like ADHD are understood and treated. Key Takeaways: Dynamic Diagnosis: Traditional one-off neuropsychological assessments can’t capture the fluctuating nature of mental health. Continuous, AI-assisted monitoring could allow clinicians to detect subtle changes in attention, emotion, and behavior, enabling earlier and more accurate interventions. Ethics and Oversight: The promise of digital twins depends on trust. Issues like privacy, bias, and informed consent are central challenges—especially when AI systems handle sensitive psychological data. Without strong governance, the same tools that personalize care could amplify inequities. Future of Mental Health Care: The authors envision a shift beyond rigid diagnostic labels like ADHD or depression toward individualized, data-driven models of well-being. If realized, this could redefine not just psychiatry but the social meaning of “mental illness” itself—ushering in both unprecedented opportunities and profound ethical debates.

    Can your “digital twin” guide your therapy? harrisonpllc.substack.com

    One of AI's most compelling advantages lies in its ability to democratize mental health support. Unlike traditional therapy services that operate within business hours, AI-powered tools offer 24/7 availability. This constant accessibility proves particularly valuable for individuals experiencing late-night anxiety episodes or those in crisis when professional help isn't immediately available. For communities in rural or underserved areas where therapy services remain scarce, AI tools can provide initial support and psychoeducation. These digital interventions can serve as crucial first steps, offering coping strategies and emotional regulation techniques when human therapists aren't geographically accessible.     In recent years, neuro feedback therapy has emerged as a highly effective tool for improving mental health and cognitive functioning. This non-invasive, evidence-based treatment method has gained popularity for its ability to address a range of neurological and psychological conditions, including anxiety, ADHD, PTSD, depression, and more. If you’re a mental health professional or someone interested in expanding your therapeutic skillset, the OSHAA 30-Hours Professional Diploma in Neurofeedback Therapy provides a comprehensive foundation in this transformative approach. Neurofeedback, also known as EEG biofeedback, is a therapeutic technique that trains individuals to regulate their brainwave activity. Through real-time monitoring and feedback, neurofeedback enables clients to learn how to optimise their brain function, improving mental clarity, emotional regulation, and overall cognitive performance. The OSHAA 30-Hours Professional Diploma in Neurofeedback Therapy is designed for professionals in the mental health and wellness sectors who want to integrate neurofeedback therapy into their practice. This diploma provides a deep understanding of the science behind neurofeedback, its applications, and how to effectively administer neurofeedback therapy to clients. The OSHAA 30-Hours Professional Diploma in Neurofeedback Therapy provides an invaluable opportunity for professionals in the mental health and wellness sectors to learn a powerful new therapeutic technique. Whether you work with clients experiencing anxiety, trauma, sleep disorders, or other cognitive challenges, neurofeedback offers a unique and effective way to promote healing and mental clarity. https://lnkd.in/ecYhavbX Occupational Safety and Health American Academy (OSHAA) #osha #oshaa #americanacademy #oshaaatp #becomeatp #award #certificate #diploma #training #education #NeurofeedbackTherapy #BrainTraining #EEGTherapy #NeuroscienceDiploma #MentalHealthTraining #ProfessionalDiploma #NeuroTherapy #ClinicalNeurofeedback #CognitiveNeurofeedback #TherapistDevelopment #IntroductionToNeurofeedbackTherapyPrinciplesAndApplications #UnderstandingBrainwavesAndTheirRoleInMentalHealth #NeurofeedbackProtocolsAndTechniques #SettingUpAndUsingNeurofeedbackEquipment #AssessingClientsForNeurofeedbackTherapy #ApplicationsOfNeurofeedbackInMentalHealthTreatment #MonitoringAndAdjustingNeurofeedbackSessions #NeurofeedbackForCognitiveEnhancementAndPeakPerformance #AdvancedTechniquesAndFutureTrendsInNeurofeedbackTherapy

    🌿 Nature and Neurodiversity: The Science Speaks 🌿 It’s no secret that being in nature feels good — but research is now showing just how profoundly it can support neurodiverse minds. In my own work — guiding leaders, teams and individuals through nature-based and futures practices — I’ve witnessed these benefits firsthand. Clarity deepens, stress softens, and focus returns. Science is catching up with what many of us have long felt: nature heals, restores, and regulates. Here’s what the evidence tells us 👇 💚 ADHD — Sharper Focus, Calmer Minds Studies show that children and adults alike with ADHD concentrate better after a walk or play in green spaces compared to built environments. 🧠 Cognitive Restoration & Memory Boosts Controlled experiments reveal that even a single nature walk can improve attention and working memory by up to 20%. 🌳 Autism Spectrum — Sensory & Social Benefits A recent meta-analysis found that nature-based interventions for autistic children improved sensory regulation, social communication, and reduced hyperactivity and irritability across several small trials. 💓 Physiological & Mental Health Gains Forest bathing and other nature-immersion practices have been linked to lower stress hormones (cortisol), reduced heart rate and blood pressure, and improved mood — all of which support emotional regulation and attention. The evidence is clear: time in nature isn’t just a nice-to-have — it’s essential neuro-nourishment. I’ve seen people leave the forest clearer, calmer, and more connected — not just to the world around them, but to themselves and others. So perhaps the real question is this: 👉 How might we better weave nature into our learning, leadership and wellbeing spaces to support all kinds of minds? Want to give it a try? Join us this weekend, links in the comments 👇 #neurodiversity #naturetherapy #wellbeing #leadership #futuresthinking #foresttherapy #naturebasedstrategy

    Addressing youth mental health challenges with precision and prudence is more vital than ever. A recent study employing the Multiphase Optimization Strategy framework reveals powerful insights into cost-effective interventions for anxiety and depression in children aged 8 to 12 years. Key takeaways indicate that long in-person group Cognitive Behavioral Therapy sessions with limited parental involvement and feedback systems provide the best value for managing depression. For anxiety, hybrid approaches with minimal parental involvement and integrated feedback offer effective, resource-conscious solutions. These findings underscore the importance of tailoring mental health strategies to balance clinical impact with economic realities—ensuring that limited resources achieve maximum emotional well-being improvements. As educators, psychologists, and policymakers strive to optimize interventions, embracing flexible delivery formats and carefully calibrated involvement can foster healthier futures for our youth. 💡 Strategic mental health investments 🤝 Collaborative, adaptive intervention models 📊 Evidence-based cost efficiency #CognitiveBehavioralTherapy #CostEffectiveCare #Education #HealthEconomics #HealthPolicy #MentalHealthInterventions #Psychology #PublicHealth #Publications #RegulatoryAgencies #YouthMentalHealth #MarketAccess #MarketAccessToday
